content:
# Luke Chapter 11 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 101-102 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_011_page_001.jpg, LUK_011_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And it came to pass, as he was in a certain place praying, when he ceased, one of his disciples said to him, "Lord, teach us to pray, even as John also taught his disciples."

**2** And he said to them, "When you pray, say: 'Father, hallowed be your name. Your kingdom come.

**3** Give us each day our daily bread.

**4** And forgive us our sins, for we ourselves also forgive everyone who is indebted to us. And lead us not into temptation.'"

**5** And he said to them, "Which of you shall have a friend, and shall go to him at midnight and say to him, 'Friend, lend me three loaves,

**6** since a friend of mine has come to me from a journey, and I have nothing to set before him';

**7** and that one from within shall answer and say, 'Do not cause me trouble; the door has already been shut, and my children are with me in bed; I am not able to rise and give to you'?

**8** I say to you, even if he will not rise and give to him because he is his friend, yet because of his persistence he will rise and give him as many as he needs.

**9** And I say to you, ask, and it shall be given to you; seek, and you shall find; knock, and it shall be opened to you.

**10** For everyone who asks receives, and the one who seeks finds, and to the one who knocks it shall be opened.

**11** And what father among you, if his son shall ask for a fish, will instead of a fish give him a serpent?

**12** Or if he shall ask for an egg, will give him a scorpion?

**13** If you then, being evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more shall the Father from heaven give the Holy Spirit to those who ask him?"

**14** And he was casting out a demon, and it was mute. And it came to pass, when the demon had gone out, the mute man spoke; and the crowds marveled.

**15** But some of them said, "By Beelzebul, the ruler of the demons, he casts out the demons."

**16** And others, testing him, were seeking from him a sign from heaven.

**17** But he, knowing their thoughts, said to them, "Every kingdom divided against itself is brought to desolation, and a house divided against a house falls.

**18** And if Satan also is divided against himself, how shall his kingdom stand? Because you say that I cast out the demons by Beelzebul.

**19** But if I by Beelzebul cast out the demons, by whom do your sons cast them out? Therefore they shall be your judges.

**20** But if by the finger of God I cast out the demons, then the kingdom of God has come upon you.

**21** When the strong man, fully armed, guards his own court, his possessions are in peace.

**22** But when one stronger than he comes upon him and overcomes him, he takes away his full armor in which he had trusted, and distributes his spoils.

**23** The one who is not with me is against me, and the one who does not gather with me scatters.

**24** When the unclean spirit has gone out of the man, it passes through waterless places seeking rest; and finding none, it says, 'I will return to my house from which I came out.'

**25** And having come, it finds it swept and put in order.

**26** Then it goes and takes along seven other spirits more evil than itself, and having entered, they dwell there; and the last state of that man becomes worse than the first."

**27** And it came to pass, as he was saying these things, a certain woman from the crowd, having lifted up her voice, said to him, "Blessed is the womb that bore you and the breasts at which you nursed."

**28** But he said, "Rather, blessed are those who hear the word of God and keep it."

**29** And as the crowds were increasing, he began to say, "This generation is an evil generation; it seeks a sign, and no sign shall be given to it except the sign of Jonah.

**30** For even as Jonah became a sign to the Ninevites, so also shall the Son of Man be to this generation.

**31** The queen of the south shall rise up in the judgment with the men of this generation and shall condemn them, for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon; and behold, something greater than Solomon is here.

**32** The men of Nineveh shall stand up in the judgment with this generation and shall condemn it, for they repented at the preaching of Jonah; and behold, something greater than Jonah is here.

**33** No one, having lit a lamp, puts it in a hidden place nor under the bushel, but upon the lampstand, that those who enter may see the light.

**34** The lamp of the body is your eye. When your eye is sound, your whole body also is full of light; but when it is evil, your body also is full of darkness.

**35** Watch therefore, lest the light that is in you be darkness.

**36** If therefore your whole body is full of light, having no part dark, it shall be wholly full of light, as when the lamp with its brightness gives you light."

**37** And as he was speaking, a Pharisee asked him to dine with him; and having entered, he reclined at table.

**38** But the Pharisee, having seen it, marveled that he had not first washed before the meal.

**39** But the Lord said to him, "Now you Pharisees cleanse the outside of the cup and of the dish, but the inside of you is full of plunder and wickedness.

**40** Fools! Did not the one who made the outside also make the inside?

**41** But give as alms the things that are within, and behold, all things are clean to you.

**42** But woe to you Pharisees! For you tithe mint and rue and every herb, and you pass by justice and the love of God; but these things it was needful to do, and those not to leave undone.

**43** Woe to you Pharisees! For you love the chief seat in the synagogues and the greetings in the marketplaces.

**44** Woe to you! For you are as the unseen tombs, and the men who walk over them do not know it."

**45** And one of the lawyers, answering, said to him, "Teacher, in saying these things you insult us also."

**46** And he said, "Woe to you lawyers also! For you load men with burdens hard to bear, and you yourselves do not touch the burdens with one of your fingers.

**47** Woe to you! For you build the tombs of the prophets, and your fathers killed them.

**48** Therefore you are witnesses and consent to the works of your fathers; for they indeed killed them, and you build their tombs.

**49** On account of this also the Wisdom of God said, 'I will send to them prophets and apostles, and some of them they shall kill and persecute,'

**50** that the blood of all the prophets, which has been shed from the foundation of the world, may be required of this generation,

**51** from the blood of Abel to the blood of Zechariah, who perished between the altar and the house. Yes, I say to you, it shall be required of this generation.

**52** Woe to you lawyers! For you have taken away the key of knowledge; you yourselves did not enter, and those who were entering you hindered."

**53** And when he had gone out from there, the scribes and the Pharisees began to press upon him vehemently and to provoke him to speak about many things,

**54** lying in wait for him, to catch something out of his mouth.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus)

1. **11:2-4 — The Lord's Prayer (shorter form):** Sinaiticus preserves the distinctly shorter Lukan form of the Lord's Prayer. Absent from Sinaiticus are the petitions "Our Father who art in heaven," "Your will be done, as in heaven so also upon earth," and "but deliver us from evil," all of which appear in the Matthean version (Matt 6:9-13) and were harmonized into Luke in later manuscripts. The Sinaiticus reading begins simply with ΠΑΤΕΡ ("Father") without the possessive or heavenly qualifier. This shorter form is followed as the earlier and more difficult reading.

2. **11:2 — "Father" (Pater) without "our" or "who art in heaven":** The bare vocative ΠΑΤΕΡ in Sinaiticus is theologically significant. Luke's shorter address reflects the Aramaic ABBA tradition and stands in deliberate contrast to the more liturgically developed Matthean form. The formal equivalent "Father" is rendered without expansion.

3. **11:4 — "sins" (hamartias) rather than "debts":** Sinaiticus reads ΑΜΑΡΤΙΑΣ ("sins") where Matthew has ΟΦΕΙΛΗΜΑΤΑ ("debts"). Luke then uses the cognate ΟΦΕΙΛΟΝΤΙ ("is indebted") in the second clause, creating a deliberate theological bridge between the concepts of sin and debt. Both terms are rendered with their formal equivalents.

4. **11:8 — "persistence" (anaideian):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΝΑΙΔΕΙΑΝ, a term meaning "shamelessness" or "persistence" — literally "lack of shame." This is the only New Testament occurrence of this word. Rendered "persistence" to capture the positive connotation in context, though the Greek more literally denotes impudent boldness. The parable turns on this quality as the ground of effective prayer.

5. **11:11 — Sinaiticus omission of "bread/stone" pair:** Sinaiticus omits the first pair found in some manuscripts ("if he shall ask for bread, will give him a stone") and proceeds directly to the fish/serpent and egg/scorpion pairs. This shorter reading is followed. The omission may represent the original text before later scribes harmonized with Matthew 7:9.

6. **11:13 — "the Holy Spirit" (to Pneuma to Hagion):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΟ ΠΝΕΥΜΑ ΤΟ ΑΓΙΟΝ with the double article construction. Where Matthew 7:11 reads "good things" (ΑΓΑΘΑ), Luke's version uniquely specifies "the Holy Spirit" as the supreme gift the Father gives. This is a distinctively Lukan pneumatological emphasis, consistent with Luke's theology throughout Luke-Acts.

7. **11:14 — "and it was mute" (kai auto en kophon):** Sinaiticus reads ΚΑΙ ΑΥΤΟ ΗΝ ΚΩΦΟΝ, specifying that the demon itself was mute (neuter, agreeing with ΔΑΙΜΟΝΙΟΝ). The imperfect ΗΝ indicates the ongoing state. Some later manuscripts read this as describing the man rather than the demon; the Sinaiticus reading is followed.

8. **11:15 — "Beelzebul" (Beelzeboul):** Sinaiticus consistently spells ΒΕΕΛΖΕΒΟΥΛ throughout this passage (vv. 15, 18, 19). This spelling likely derives from the Hebrew BA'AL ZEBUL ("lord of the dwelling/height") rather than the pejorative BA'AL ZEBUB ("lord of flies") found in 2 Kings 1:2. The transliteration "Beelzebul" is maintained.

9. **11:20 — "finger of God" (daktyloi Theou):** Sinaiticus reads ΔΑΚΤΥΛΩ ΘΕΟΥ where the Matthean parallel (Matt 12:28) has ΠΝΕΥΜΑΤΙ ΘΕΟΥ ("Spirit of God"). Luke's "finger of God" is an Exodus allusion (Exod 8:19), connecting Jesus's exorcisms to the liberation from Pharaoh. This is rendered literally as "the finger of God."

10. **11:22 — "his full armor" (ten panoplian):** The term ΠΑΝΟΠΛΙΑΝ ("full armor, panoply") appears only here and in Ephesians 6:11, 13 in the New Testament. It denotes the complete suit of a heavily armed soldier. Rendered "his full armor" to preserve the military imagery of the parable.

11. **11:27 — "a certain woman from the crowd" (gyne tis ek tou ochlou):** Sinaiticus reads ΓΥΝΗ ΤΙΣ ΕΚ ΤΟΥ ΟΧΛΟΥ. The phrase "having lifted up her voice" (ΕΠΑΡΑΣΑ ΦΩΝΗΝ) is a Semitic idiom for speaking loudly or publicly. The unnamed woman's beatitude on Mary's physical motherhood is redirected by Jesus to the hearing and keeping of God's word.

12. **11:31 — "something greater" (pleion):** The neuter comparative ΠΛΕΙΟΝ ("something greater") rather than the masculine "someone greater" is significant. Jesus does not merely say he is greater than Solomon or Jonah as a person, but that something qualitatively greater — the kingdom itself — is present. The neuter is preserved in translation.

13. **11:33 — "hidden place" (krypten):** Sinaiticus reads ΚΡΥΠΤΗΝ ("hidden place, cellar, crypt") where some manuscripts omit this word. The double negative location ("not in a hidden place nor under the bushel") emphasizes the absurdity of concealing light. The Sinaiticus fuller reading is followed.

14. **11:34 — "sound" (haplous):** The adjective ΑΠΛΟΥΣ literally means "single, simple, generous." In the context of the eye as the body's lamp, it denotes an eye that is healthy, clear, and undivided in its vision. Rendered "sound" as the formal equivalent, though the Greek carries overtones of moral simplicity and generosity.

15. **11:39 — "plunder and wickedness" (harpages kai ponerias):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΡΠΑΓΗΣ ΚΑΙ ΠΟΝΗΡΙΑΣ ("plunder and wickedness") where Matthew 23:25 has "extortion and self-indulgence." Luke's pairing emphasizes the violent acquisition (ΑΡΠΑΓΗ, "seizure, plunder") and moral corruption (ΠΟΝΗΡΙΑ, "wickedness") underlying the Pharisees' outward piety.

16. **11:41 — "give as alms the things that are within" (ta enonta dote eleemosynen):** This notoriously difficult Sinaiticus reading, ΤΑ ΕΝΟΝΤΑ ΔΟΤΕ ΕΛΕΗΜΟΣΥΝΗΝ, is rendered formally. The phrase TA ENONTA ("the things within") may refer to the contents of the cup and dish (give as alms what is inside), or to inner disposition. The formal translation preserves the ambiguity of the Greek.

17. **11:44 — "unseen tombs" (ta mnemeia ta adela):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΑ ΜΝΗΜΕΙΑ ΤΑ ΑΔΗΛΑ ("the unseen/unmarked tombs"). In Jewish practice, contact with a grave produced ritual impurity (Num 19:16). The comparison is devastating: the Pharisees defile others unknowingly. Matthew 23:27 has "whitewashed tombs" — a different but related image. Luke's version emphasizes the hidden, undetectable nature of the contamination.

18. **11:49 — "the Wisdom of God said" (he Sophia tou Theou eipen):** Sinaiticus reads Η ΣΟΦΙΑ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ ΕΙΠΕΝ. This formula introduces a prophetic oracle attributed to divine Wisdom personified. No exact source in the Old Testament matches the quotation that follows. Some scholars identify this as a lost wisdom writing; others understand Jesus as speaking as the Wisdom of God incarnate. The formal equivalent preserves the attribution without interpretive expansion.

19. **11:51 — "the house" (tou oikou):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΟΥ ΟΙΚΟΥ ("the house") rather than ΤΟΥ ΝΑΟΥ ("the temple") found in some manuscripts. Both refer to the temple complex, but ΟΙΚΟΣ may echo the language of 2 Chronicles 24:20-21, where Zechariah the son of Jehoiada was killed in the court of the Lord's house. The Sinaiticus reading is followed.

20. **11:53-54 — "press upon him vehemently" (deinōs enechein):** The adverb ΔΕΙΝΩΣ ("terribly, vehemently") combined with ΕΝΕΧΕΙΝ ("to hold against, press upon") conveys intense hostile pressure. The infinitive ΑΠΟΣΤΟΜΑΤΙΖΕΙΝ ("to provoke to speak, to catechize") in verse 53 is a rare term suggesting interrogation or forcing someone to speak extemporaneously, rendered "to provoke him to speak." The chapter closes with a dramatic escalation of hostility.

### Translation Philosophy

1. **Formal equivalence in prayer language:** The Lord's Prayer (vv. 2-4) is rendered with strict formal equivalence to the Sinaiticus text, resisting the strong temptation to harmonize with the longer, more familiar Matthean form. The sparse, direct quality of Luke's version — "Father" rather than "Our Father who art in heaven" — is preserved as a witness to the earlier textual tradition.

2. **Parabolic discourse register:** The friend at midnight parable (vv. 5-8), the strong man parable (vv. 21-22), and the return of the unclean spirit (vv. 24-26) are rendered in a narrative register that preserves the rhetorical force of Jesus's teaching. Interrogative structures are maintained where the Greek uses them to draw listeners into the argument.

3. **Prophetic woe-oracle form preserved:** The six woes (vv. 42-44, 46-47, 52) are rendered with the formulaic "Woe to you" (ΟΥΑΙ ΥΜΙΝ) preserved consistently. The prophetic oracle form — accusation followed by reason introduced by "for" (HOTI) — mirrors the structure of Old Testament prophetic indictments (cf. Isa 5:8-23; Amos 6:1).

4. **Theological vocabulary maintained:** Key terms are rendered consistently: kingdom of God (HE BASILEIA TOU THEOU), the Holy Spirit (TO PNEUMA TO HAGION), Son of Man (HO HUIOS TOU ANTHROPOU), the word of God (HO LOGOS TOU THEOU). The title "Lord" (KYRIOS) applied to Jesus at v. 39 is preserved, reflecting Luke's Christological usage.

5. **Sinaiticus priority throughout:** Where Sinaiticus diverges from later manuscripts, the Sinaiticus reading is followed: the shorter Lord's Prayer (vv. 2-4), the omission of the bread/stone pair (v. 11), "the house" rather than "the temple" (v. 51), and the fuller reading at v. 33 including "hidden place."

6. **Septuagintal echoes preserved:** Luke 11 is rich in Old Testament allusions: "finger of God" (Exod 8:19), "blood of Abel" (Gen 4:10), "blood of Zechariah" (2 Chr 24:20-22), "queen of the south" (1 Kgs 10:1-13), and the sign of Jonah (Jonah 1-3). The formal translation preserves the language that connects these allusions to their Septuagintal sources.

7. **Consistency with Luke 10:** The translation maintains continuity in vocabulary, register, and approach with the preceding chapter. The transition from Martha's house (10:38-42) to prayer instruction (11:1) continues Luke's thematic arc connecting discipleship, listening to the word, and prayer.
